question: Using Tables 5.1-1 and 5.1-2, what is the relationship between the RCS hot leg volume and the hot leg temperature?

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: This section describes the evaluation of the NuScale Power Plant's system performance, focusing on optimizing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressure to maximize electrical generation and maintain safety margins. Calculations using NRELAP5 software, supported by hand calculations, account for uncertainties and ensure efficient natural circulation with minimal pressure losses, while design considerations exclude steam generator tube plugging from affecting primary coolant flow.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system. It specifies the types of steel and alloys used for various parts, their compliance with relevant BPVC material specifications, and highlights corrosion protection measures such as weld cladding with stainless steel and nickel-based alloys.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The section describes the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) of the NuScale Power Module, highlighting its role in removing noncondensible gases and maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary. The RCS, located inside the containment vessel, transfers heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and interfaces with several other systems for control, safety, and sampling.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes key components of the NuScale reactor system. It details the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core and supports major systems, and outlines the main Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping and its connections. The section also briefly introduces the pressurizer (PZR), which is integral to the RPV.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering unnecessary reactor trips or safety system actuations. Instrumentation provides real-time pressure and level data to plant monitoring and control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and decay heat from the reactor core during non-LOCA events when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours after an event, protecting fuel and pressure boundaries, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure-retaining component and barrier to fission product release, supporting various reactor systems and components, and is constructed and certified according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure integrity throughout the plant's design lifetime. Material specifications for the RPV and its components are selected to maintain safety and compliance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and its reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with relevant U.S. nuclear regulations, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria. It details that these components are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III standards, with specific classifications for different piping systems, and references the applicable ASME BPVC edition and regulatory guides used for certification.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design emphasizes maintaining coolant quality, uniform boron concentration, and preventing fuel design limit violations during all operating conditions. Each NuScale Power Module (NPM) operates independently, with the RCS forming a key subsystem for plant safety and performance.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards used for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspection and testing of reactor coolant pressure boundary components are performed according to the latest applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

edges.description: Section A discusses how steady state values for primary system parameters, such as hot leg volume and temperature, are established to optimize plant performance, while Section B provides the design basis and configuration of the RCS, which determines the physical parameters (including hot leg volume) that influence those steady state values.

Section A discusses how steady state values for primary system parameters, such as hot leg temperature, are established as a function of reactor power to optimize performance, while Section B describes the physical configuration and functions of the RCS, including the hot leg volume, indicating that the hot leg volume (from Section B) is a design parameter that influences or is associated with the hot leg temperature (from Section A) in system evaluations.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B references specific components and materials of the pressurizer, with Section B citing details from other sections that are part of the broader system described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies operational features such as degasification and pressure boundary integrity, both of which are influenced by RCS hot leg volume and temperature as detailed in Tables 5.1-1 and 5.1-2.

Section B discusses how steady state values, such as hot leg temperature, are established as a function of reactor power and system parameters like RCS hot leg volume, which are described in Section A as part of the system components.

Section A describes operational procedures involving RCS components, while Section B provides the general design basis and configuration of the RCS, establishing the context for understanding the specific component operations mentioned in Section A.

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and function within the reactor vessel, while Section B provides a general overview of the RCS design and its components, including the pressurizer, thus Section A specifies details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A describes the functional design and role of the pressurizer in the reactor coolant system, while Section B provides references to the materials and components described in Section A, specifically citing where further material details can be found.

Section A describes operational procedures involving the reactor coolant system (RCS) and pressurizer, while Section B details the design and function of the pressurizer as part of the RCS, with both sections connected by their focus on the interaction and pressure communication between the RCS and pressurizer.

Section A describes the DHRS's function to remove decay heat from the RCS to maintain safe conditions, while Section B explains that steady-state parameters like hot leg volume and temperature are established as a function of reactor power to optimize system performance, connecting the two by showing that the DHRS's effectiveness depends on these RCS parameters.

Section A describes the function and design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in maintaining safe RCS conditions, while Section B provides a general overview of the RCS components and their reliance on natural circulation, with both sections referencing the RCS and its operational parameters as foundational context.

Section A describes the physical configuration of the reactor coolant system (RCS), including the hot leg as part of the reactor vessel, while Section B discusses how steady-state parameters such as hot leg volume and temperature are established and evaluated for optimal system performance, thus connecting the physical design (A) to the operational evaluation (B).

Section A describes the physical components of the reactor vessel, which includes the hot leg volume as part of the RCS, while Section B provides the design basis and operational context for the RCS, establishing the importance of parameters like hot leg temperature in system performance.

Section A describes the reactor vessel as part of the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B details the pressurizer components, both of which are subsystems within the RCS and are referenced in each other's descriptions, indicating a structural relationship through cross-referencing of system components.

Section B discusses how steady state values for primary system parameters, such as RCS hot leg volume and temperature, are established to optimize plant performance, while Section A ensures that these design values comply with regulatory codes and standards.

Section B references Section A by indicating that material descriptions for the pressurizer components are provided in Section 5.2, thereby linking the detailed component materials to the compliance requirements outlined in Section A.

Section B discusses how steady state values for primary system parameters, such as hot leg temperature, are established as a function of reactor power and system design (which includes hot leg volume described in Section A), indicating that the physical configuration (volume) in Section A influences the operational parameter (temperature) in Section B.
Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and its components, while Section B gives specific material details about the pressurizer, which is a component described in Section A.

Section A discusses the materials and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners in the RCS, while Section B provides a general summary of the RCS design and its components, with Section A referencing Section B for further design details.

Section A references Section B by directing the reader to Section 3.13 for further details on the design of threaded fasteners, while Section B similarly references Section 5.2 for material descriptions, indicating a cross-referential structural relationship.

Section A describes the physical configuration and components of the reactor coolant system (RCS), including the reactor vessel and its connection to the hot leg, while Section B establishes that these components, including the hot leg, must comply with regulatory codes such as ASME BPVC, thus linking the design details in A to the regulatory requirements in B.

Section A describes the reactor vessel as a component of the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B provides a summary of the RCS, explicitly stating that the RCS includes the reactor pressure vessel described in Section A.

Section A describes the overall configuration and components of the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B details the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners used in RCS pressure boundary components, connecting them through their shared focus on RCS integrity and design.

Section A states that the RCS hot leg volume and temperature must comply with ASME and regulatory requirements, while Section B provides a general description of the RCS design, including its reliance on natural circulation, which is influenced by hot leg volume and temperature.

Section A states that all reactor coolant pressure boundary components, including those discussed in Section B (such as threaded fasteners), are required to comply with ASME BPVC Section III, and Section B specifies that the threaded fasteners indeed conform to these code requirements.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B details the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners within the RCS, with both sections connected by their focus on different aspects of the RCS's integrity and operation.
